arguments.command: which llvm-symbolizer addr2line 2>/dev/null; dpkg -l | grep -i llvm 2>/dev/null | head -5

data.reasoningText:
The user wants me to symbolicate the .NET runtime frames in this tombstone. The key frames are from `libcoreclr.so` with BuildId `4ca7e2f19b3d605847a1c8e3f5b7d9a2c4e6f801`.

To symbolicate these, I need to:
1. Find the debug symbols for `libcoreclr.so` with that specific BuildId
2. Use a tool like `llvm-symbolizer` or `addr2line` to resolve the addresses to function names

The BuildId is `4ca7e2f19b3d605847a1c8e3f5b7d9a2c4e6f801`. 

Since this is a .NET runtime library, I can try downloading the debug symbols from Microsoft's symbol server. The typical URL pattern for ELF files uses the BuildId in the path, so I'd construct a URL with the filename and BuildId to fetch the corresponding debug information. I should try downloading the debug symbols directly from Microsoft's symbol server using the standard ELF BuildId URL format, then use tools like llvm-symbolizer or addr2line to resolve the addresses.

arguments.command: which llvm-symbolizer addr2line 2>/dev/null; dpkg -l | grep -i llvm 2>/dev/null | head -5

toolRequests.name: bash

arguments.command:
BUILDID="4ca7e2f19b3d605847a1c8e3f5b7d9a2c4e6f801"
# Try downloading from Microsoft symbol server - ELF format
mkdir -p /tmp/symbols
# Try the standard pattern
curl -sf -o /tmp/symbols/libcoreclr.so "https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/libcoreclr.so/elf-buildid-${BUILDID}/libcoreclr.so" && echo "Downloaded binary" || echo "Binary download failed"
# Try debug symbol pattern
curl -sf -o /tmp/symbols/libcoreclr.so.dbg "https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/_.debug/elf-buildid-sym-${BUILDID}/_.debug" && echo "Downloaded debug symbols" || echo "Debug symbols download failed"
# Try alternate debug pattern
curl -sf -o /tmp/symbols/libcoreclr.so.dwarf "https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/libcoreclr.so.dwarf/elf-buildid-sym-${BUILDID}/libcoreclr.so.dwarf" && echo "Downloaded dwarf" || echo "Dwarf download failed"
arguments.description: Download symbols from Microsoft symbol server

  "libcoreclr.so/${BUILDID}/libcoreclr.so" \

done

arguments.command:
# Check if dotnet is available
which dotnet 2>/dev/null && dotnet --version || echo "dotnet not available"
# Also try debuginfod
BUILDID="4ca7e2f19b3d605847a1c8e3f5b7d9a2c4e6f801"
curl -sf -o /dev/null -w "HTTP %{http_code}" "https://debuginfod.elfutils.org/buildid/${BUILDID}/debuginfo" && echo " (debuginfod)" || echo ""
# Try NuGet symbol server too
curl -sf -o /dev/null -w "HTTP %{http_code}" "https://symbols.nuget.org/download/symbols/libcoreclr.so/elf-buildid-${BUILDID}/libcoreclr.so" && echo " (nuget symbols)" || echo ""
curl -sf -o /dev/null -w "HTTP %{http_code}" "https://symbols.nuget.org/download/symbols/libcoreclr.so/elf-buildid-sym-${BUILDID}/libcoreclr.so" && echo " (nuget symbols sym)" || echo ""
arguments.description: Check dotnet CLI and alternative symbol servers

toolRequests.intentionSummary: Check dotnet CLI and alternative symbol servers

data.reasoningText:
The BuildId doesn't seem to correspond to a real .NET runtime build on Microsoft's symbol server. This is likely a synthetic/sample tombstone file.

Let me try to install the `dotnet-symbol` tool which is the official Microsoft tool for downloading symbols. Or maybe I should try the NuGet-based approach where I can get runtime symbols from a specific .NET release.

Actually, let me step back. The BuildId `4ca7e2f19b3d605847a1c8e3f5b7d9a2c4e6f801` is 40 hex chars = 20 bytes, which is the right size for a SHA1 build ID. But it may be a fabricated one for this sample.


Since this appears to be a sample file, I'm wondering if I can resolve these addresses without downloading symbols at all—maybe by checking if the CoreCLR runtime has publicly available symbol files bundled with the .NET SDK or runtime packages. I could try using the dotnet-symbol tool to download symbols, though I'd need the actual binary first.

For .NET Android specifically, the symbols are distributed differently than the standard .NET runtime—they're part of the Microsoft.NETCore.App.Runtime.android-arm64 NuGet package. Without knowing which .NET version this corresponds to, and given that the BuildId seems synthetic, I should probably be upfront with the user about these limitations. Let me still try a few more approaches though, starting with dotnet-symbol if it's available.

Though realistically, this tombstone file probably has a synthetic BuildId, meaning no real symbols exist for it anyway. Let me try installing the necessary tools and see what's possible.

toolResult.textResultForLlm:
Tool 'dotnet-symbol' is already installed.

Usage: dotnet symbol [options] <FILES>

Arguments:
  <FILES>   List of files. Can contain wildcards.

Options:
  --microsoft-symbol-server                         Add 'https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols' symbol server path (default).
  --server-path <symbol server path>                Add a http server path.
  --authenticated-server-path <pat> <server path>   Add a http PAT authenticated server path.
  --cache-directory <file cache directory>          Add a cache directory.
  --timeout <minutes>                               Change http timeout in minutes (default: 4).
  --recurse-subdirectories                          Process input files in all subdirectories.
  --host-only                                       Download only the host program (i.e. dotnet) that lldb needs for loading coredumps.
  --symbols                                         Download the symbol files (.pdb, .dbg, .dwarf).
  --modules                                         Download the module files (.dll, .so, .dylib).
  --debugging                                       Download the special debugging modules (DAC, DBI, SOS).
  --windows-pdbs                                    Force the downloading of the Windows PDBs when Portable PDBs are also available.
  --overwrite                                       Overwrite existing files in output directory.
  --verifycore                                      Verify ELF core dump.
<exited with exit code 0>

toolRequests.name: bash

arguments.command:
BUILDID="4ca7e2f19b3d605847a1c8e3f5b7d9a2c4e6f801"
# Try uppercase build ID
BUILDID_UPPER=$(echo "$BUILDID" | tr 'a-f' 'A-F')
# Try a few more patterns on MS symbol server
for pattern in \
  "libcoreclr.so/elf-buildid-${BUILDID_UPPER}/libcoreclr.so" \
  "libcoreclr.so.dbg/elf-buildid-sym-${BUILDID_UPPER}/libcoreclr.so.dbg" \
  "libcoreclr.so/${BUILDID}/libcoreclr.so.dbg" \
  "coreclr.so/elf-buildid-${BUILDID}/coreclr.so"; do
  code=$(curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" "https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/${pattern}")
  echo "HTTP $code: $pattern"
done
# Also try dotnet symbol server
for pattern in \
  "libcoreclr.so/elf-buildid-${BUILDID}/libcoreclr.so" \
  "libcoreclr.so.dbg/elf-buildid-sym-${BUILDID}/libcoreclr.so.dbg"; do
  code=$(curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" "https://dotnetbuilds.blob.core.windows.net/dbgsymbols/${pattern}")
  echo "HTTP $code (dotnetbuilds): $pattern"
done

  "libcoreclr.so/elf-buildid-${BUILDID_UPPER}/libcoreclr.so" \
  "libcoreclr.so.dbg/elf-buildid-sym-${BUILDID_UPPER}/libcoreclr.so.dbg" \
  "libcoreclr.so/${BUILDID}/libcoreclr.so.dbg" \
  "coreclr.so/elf-buildid-${BUILDID}/coreclr.so"; do
  code=$(curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" "https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/${pattern}")
  echo "HTTP $code: $pattern"
done
# Also try dotnet symbol server
for pattern in \
  "libcoreclr.so/elf-buildid-${BUILDID}/libcoreclr.so" \
  "libcoreclr.so.dbg/elf-buildid-sym-${BUILDID}/libcoreclr.so.dbg"; do
  code=$(curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" "https://dotnetbuilds.blob.core.windows.net/dbgsymbols/${pattern}")
  echo "HTTP $code (dotnetbuilds): $pattern"
done

1. **From your build output**: Find `libcoreclr.so` (with symbols) in your app's `obj/` directory or the .NET SDK pack (`packs/Microsoft.NETCore.App.Runtime.android-arm64/`)
2. **Using `dotnet-symbol`**:

3. **Then resolve addresses**:
   ```
   addr2line -e libcoreclr.so -f 0x3a81f0 0x3a9244 0x298b10 0x298c48 0x18e3a4 0x18df6c
   ```
